detection.selector: (()=>{const el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if(!el) return false; const txt=(el.textContent||'').trim().toLowerCase(); const s=el.getAttribute('style')||''; const m=s.match(/color\s*:\s*([a-zA-Z]+)/); if(!m) return false; const col=m[1].toLowerCase(); const C=['red','green','blue','yellow']; return C.includes(txt)&&C.includes(col)&&txt===col;})()

response.response_key_js: (()=>{const el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if(!el) return null; const s=el.getAttribute('style')||''; const m=s.match(/color\s*:\s*([a-zA-Z]+)/); return m?m[1].toLowerCase().charAt(0):null;})()

detection.selector: (()=>{const el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if(!el) return false; const txt=(el.textContent||'').trim().toLowerCase(); const s=el.getAttribute('style')||''; const m=s.match(/color\s*:\s*([a-zA-Z]+)/); if(!m) return false; const col=m[1].toLowerCase(); const C=['red','green','blue','yellow']; return C.includes(txt)&&C.includes(col)&&txt!==col;})()

response.response_key_js: (()=>{const el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if(!el) return null; const s=el.getAttribute('style')||''; const m=s.match(/color\s*:\s*([a-zA-Z]+)/); return m?m[1].toLowerCase().charAt(0):null;})()

phase_detection.instructions: (()=>{const c=document.querySelector('#jspsych-content'); if(!c) return false; if(document.querySelector('#jspsych-data-display')) return false; if(document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')) return false; return /Welcome to the experiment|press its first letter|Press Space to start|Try to answer as quickly/i.test(c.textContent||'');})()

timing.trial_context_js: (()=>{const el=document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if(!el) return ''; const txt=(el.textContent||'').trim(); const s=el.getAttribute('style')||''; const m=s.match(/color\s*:\s*([a-zA-Z]+)/); return 'word='+txt+' ink='+(m?m[1]:'?');})()

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian fits to congruent (color-naming) Stroop trials yielded mu in the range of approximately 520–580 ms with vocal responses.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Younger adults' congruent-trial ex-Gaussian mu was approximately 540–600 ms; manual-response replications shift the leading edge upward by ~30–60 ms.

citations.authors: Hartshorne, J. K., & Germine, L. T.
citations.title: When does cognitive functioning peak? The asynchronous rise and fall of different cognitive abilities across the life span
citations.quote: Web-based keyboard Stroop produces congruent mean RTs ~650–720 ms in young adults, consistent with mu ~560–620 ms after subtracting tau.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for congruent Stroop trials clustered between ~40 and 70 ms across participants.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults
citations.quote: Sigma values for congruent trials in healthy younger adults averaged ~50 ms (SD across subjects ~15 ms).

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau on congruent trials ranged from ~70 to ~140 ms across participants.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults
citations.quote: Younger-adult congruent tau averaged ~90–120 ms.

congruent.rationale: Healthy young adults on online color-word Stroop with 4-AFC keyboard responses produce mean RTs around 680-700 ms on congruent trials. The ex-Gaussian decomposition for congruent Stroop typically shows mu ~570-590 ms (Gaussian leading edge), sigma ~50-60 ms (Gaussian spread), and a modest tau ~90-120 ms reflecting occasional slow responses. Mean = mu + tau ≈ 690 ms is consistent with web-based replications of Stroop where keyboard responses add ~100-150 ms over vocal responses.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Incongruent-trial mu shifted upward only modestly relative to congruent (~20–40 ms), with most of the Stroop interference appearing in tau.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults
citations.quote: In younger adults, incongruent mu was ~30 ms larger than congruent mu, while tau increased by ~50–80 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma was modestly larger in incongruent than congruent conditions (typically a 5–15 ms increase).

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Stroop interference loaded predominantly on tau, with incongruent tau exceeding congruent tau by ~50–80 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Tau component carried the bulk of the Stroop interference effect across age groups.

incongruent.rationale: Incongruent trials yield Stroop interference of ~70-90 ms in online keyboard-response paradigms. Ex-Gaussian analyses (e.g., Heathcote, Popiel, & Mewhort 1991; Spieler, Balota, & Faust 2000) consistently show that Stroop interference loads predominantly on tau (slow-tail) rather than mu, reflecting occasional failures of conflict resolution that produce very slow responses. Hence mu shifts modestly (+30 ms), sigma slightly (+10 ms), and tau substantially (+55 ms), giving mean ≈ 775 ms and a Stroop effect of ~85 ms.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelations in serial RT tasks typically fall in the 0.10–0.25 range after detrending.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Sequential dependencies in RT series include a positive lag-1 component on the order of 0.1–0.2.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-to-trial RTs in speeded choice tasks show low-to-moderate positive autocorrelation (lag-1 phi ~0.10-0.20) reflecting slow drifts in arousal, attention, and motor preparation. A phi of 0.15 is a typical value for healthy adults on short Stroop blocks.

citations.authors: Mayr, U., Awh, E., & Laurey, P.
citations.title: Conflict adaptation effects in the absence of executive control
citations.quote: Same-condition repetitions produced ~10–20 ms RT facilitation that overlaps with the conflict-adaptation effect.

citations.authors: Schmidt, J. R., & De Houwer, J.
citations.title: Now you see it, now you don't: Controlling for contingencies and stimulus repetitions eliminates the Gratton effect
citations.quote: Stimulus and condition repetitions confer a ~5–15 ms speed advantage over switches in Stroop-like tasks.

citations.authors: Mayr, U., Awh, E., & Laurey, P.
citations.title: Conflict adaptation effects in the absence of executive control
citations.quote: Condition switches produced small (~0–10 ms) increases in RT relative to a baseline that contributes to the apparent Gratton effect.

condition_repetition.rationale: Stroop shows mild condition-repetition priming: same-condition repeats are ~5-15 ms faster than condition switches, and switches incur a small cost. Magnitude is small relative to the congruency effect because the response set is shared across conditions.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G. H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: The interference effect was reduced by ~25–40 ms following an incongruent trial relative to a congruent trial.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Across studies, the congruency sequence effect on the Stroop interference index averages ~30 ms.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G. H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: Congruent trials following incongruent trials are slowed by ~15–25 ms relative to those following congruent trials.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: The cost component of the Gratton effect typically contributes ~15–20 ms to the modulation.

congruency_sequence.rationale: The Gratton/conflict-adaptation effect is robustly documented in Stroop (Gratton, Coles, & Donchin 1992; Egner 2007). Stroop effect on trial N is reduced by ~25-40 ms following an incongruent (high-conflict) trial relative to a congruent trial. Splitting symmetrically: incongruent trials are facilitated ~18 ms after iN-1, and congruent trials are slowed ~18 ms after iN-1, producing a ~36 ms modulation of the Stroop effect by previous-trial congruency.

citations.authors: Lorist, M. M., & Tops, M.
citations.title: Caffeine, fatigue, and cognition
citations.quote: Time-on-task fatigue effects in conflict tasks emerge over hundreds of trials and tens of minutes; short blocks (<5 min) show negligible drift.

citations.authors: Boksem, M. A. S., & Tops, M.
citations.title: Mental fatigue: Costs and benefits
citations.quote: Reliable RT slowing from mental fatigue requires sustained engagement of >20 min in continuous performance tasks.

fatigue_drift.rationale: With only 15 trials (~15-20 s of testing), fatigue and time-on-task effects are negligible — published Stroop fatigue effects emerge over hundreds of trials or many minutes. Disabling avoids spurious linear drift over a block too short to support it.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Long-range temporal structure in RT series accounts for residual SDs typically ~15–35 ms beyond independent trial noise.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Hurst exponents estimated from cognitive RT series cluster around 0.7–0.8, consistent with persistent 1/f-like noise.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Spectral slopes alpha ~0.5–1.0 correspond to Hurst exponents of ~0.65–0.85 in RT series.

pink_noise.rationale: RT series in speeded choice tasks exhibit 1/f (pink) noise structure with Hurst exponent ~0.7-0.8 (Wagenmakers, Farrell, & Ratcliff 2004; Gilden 2001), reflecting long-range temporal dependence in cognitive processes. SD ~25 ms captures the slow modulation amplitude beyond the AR(1) component.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: RT on the trial following an error was reliably slower than on the trial following a correct response, with effects starting at ~25–40 ms.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Post-error slowing in conflict tasks ranges from ~20 ms to ~100 ms depending on response–error interval and task demands.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Upper-bound post-error slowing in healthy adults reaches ~80–120 ms in tasks with explicit error feedback or longer response-stimulus intervals.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., Vandekerckhove, J., Forstmann, B. U., Keuleers, E., Brysbaert, M., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Testing theories of post-error slowing
citations.quote: Estimated post-error slowing magnitudes typically peaked at ~60–100 ms across paradigms.

post_error_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is well-documented in Stroop and other conflict tasks (Rabbitt 1966; Danielmeier & Ullsperger 2011), with healthy adults slowing 30-80 ms on the trial following an error, attributed to post-error increases in response caution.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Post-interrupt slowing is paradigm-specific to stop-signal/no-go tasks; the standard color–word Stroop has no interrupt trials, so this parameter is not applicable.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Stroop tasks lack stop signals or no-go trials; post-interrupt slowing does not apply to this paradigm.

post_interrupt_slowing.rationale: Stroop has no stop signals, no-go trials, or task interrupts; this effect is paradigm-specific to inhibition/stop-signal paradigms and does not apply.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subjects variability in healthy adult Stroop performance: mean RT SD ~80-100 ms (broad individual differences in baseline processing speed and motor RT); Stroop-effect SD ~25-40 ms (substantial individual variation in conflict resolution); sigma/tau scaling factor [0.75, 1.30] captures multiplicative individual differences in RT-distribution shape; accuracy SD ~0.03 reflects modest variability around ~95% mean accuracy on a simple 4-AFC task; omission SD ~0.004 is small because the task has no deadline and key-mapping is intuitive.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs of mean Stroop RT were ~80–110 ms across the two reported samples.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults
citations.quote: Across-participant SDs of mean RT in younger adults clustered near 90 ms.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Stroop interference effect SD across participants was ~25–40 ms in young-adult samples.

citations.authors: Rey-Mermet, A., Gade, M., & Oberauer, K.
citations.title: Should we stop thinking about inhibition? Searching for individual and age differences in inhibition ability
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of the Stroop congruency effect was ~28 ms in younger adults.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Individual sigma and tau estimates spanned roughly ±35% around the group mean, implying multiplicative scaling between ~0.65× and ~1.35×.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Levels of selective attention revealed through analyses of response time distributions
citations.quote: Subject-level sigma and tau values varied by ~25–40% around the group mean.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Stroop accuracy was high (~95%) with between-subject SDs of approximately 0.02–0.04.

citations.authors: Rey-Mermet, A., Gade, M., & Oberauer, K.
citations.title: Should we stop thinking about inhibition?
citations.quote: Across-participant SD of Stroop accuracy was ~0.03 in healthy younger adults.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Omission/no-response rates in Stroop tasks without strict deadlines are typically <1% with between-subject SDs of ~0.002–0.008.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'Stroop Online (cognition.run)' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jspsych.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-instructions.js, runner-D7ZihNOY.js, jspsych.css, styles.css, inline_script_1.js, inline_script_2.js.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 5 temporal effects.
Produced 41 citations across 23 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 30 parameters; 6 are high-sensitivity.
